items.title: How to Create Custom Marshaler for gRPC in Go?
items.author: Generatecode
items.content:
In software development, especially when dealing with APIs, it’s crucial to have data accurately represented according to the expected formats. In this case, you're working with gRPC and need to customize how fields are marshaled to JSON. If your application exchanges data including SHA-1, SHA-256, and UUIDs as , you might find the default behavior of converting to base64 rather limiting. Let’s explore how to implement a custom marshaler in Go to handle this effectively, ensuring that your specific use case can accommodate hexadecimal encoding instead of base64.

The default behavior for handling in gRPC-Gateway is to convert them into base64 later when transforming them into JSON. This decision can be frustrating when hexadecimal representations are needed. Using hexadecimal encoding is common for cryptographic hashes and UUIDs, making it more human-readable and often easier to handle than base64 encoded strings. Therefore, you want to establish a way to customize the marshaling process while keeping your proto file structure intact, leveraging for clarity.

Steps for Creating a Custom Marshaler

To customize the marshaling of your fields, follow these steps:

Step 1: Implementing the Struct

When creating your custom marshaler, you’ll be extending the existing struct from the gRPC-Gateway. Here is how to set it up:

package service

import (
    "myapp/api/v1/hashes"
    "fmt"

    "encoding/base64"
    "encoding/hex"
    "encoding/json"

    "github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2/runtime"
    "google.golang.org/protobuf/encoding/protojson"
    "google.golang.org/protobuf/proto"
    "google.golang.org/protobuf/reflect/protoreflect"
    "google.golang.org/protobuf/types/descriptorpb"
)

type HexBytesJSONPb struct {
    runtime.JSONPb
}

Step 2: Overriding the Marshal Method

The next step is to override the function. This function converts your proto message to JSON, and we need to ensure it handles fields marked for hexadecimal encoding properly:

func (m *HexBytesJSONPb) Marshal(v interface{}) ([]byte, error) {
    msg, ok := v.(proto.Message)
    if !ok {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("message is not proto.Message")
    }

    marshaled, err := protojson.MarshalOptions{
        EmitUnpopulated: true,
        UseProtoNames:   true,
    }.Marshal(msg)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }

    var jsonObj map[string]interface{}
    if err := json.Unmarshal(marshaled, &jsonObj); err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }

    m.overrideBytesToHex(msg.ProtoReflect(), jsonObj)

    return json.Marshal(jsonObj)
}

Step 3: Implementing Helper Functions

Two helper functions are essential: for converting fields from base64 to hexadecimal, and for reconverting them accordingly:

func (m *HexBytesJSONPb) overrideBytesToHex(msg protoreflect.Message, jsonMap map[string]interface{}) {
    msg.Range(func(fd protoreflect.FieldDescriptor, v protoreflect.Value) bool {
        if fd.Kind() == protoreflect.BytesKind {
            opts := fd.Options().(*descriptorpb.FieldOptions)
            if proto.GetExtension(opts, hashes.E_JsonHex).(bool) {
                fieldName := fd.JSONName()
                if raw, ok := jsonMap[fieldName]; ok {
                    if base64Str, ok := raw.(string); ok {
                        decoded, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(base64Str)
                        if err == nil {
                            jsonMap[fieldName] = hex.EncodeToString(decoded)
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return true
    })
}

func (m *HexBytesJSONPb) overrideHexToBase64(msg protoreflect.Message, jsonMap map[string]interface{}) {
    msg.Range(func(fd protoreflect.FieldDescriptor, _ protoreflect.Value) bool {
        if fd.Kind() == protoreflect.BytesKind {
            opts := fd.Options().(*descriptorpb.FieldOptions)
            ext := proto.GetExtension(opts, hashes.E_JsonHex)
            if mark, ok := ext.(bool); ok && mark {
                fieldName := fd.JSONName()
                if raw, ok := jsonMap[fieldName].(string); ok {
                    decoded, err := hex.DecodeString(raw)
                    if err == nil {
                        jsonMap[fieldName] = base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(decoded)
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return true
    })
}

Step 4: Handling Unmarshalling

Next, ensure that your method works properly. This is crucial for converting JSON back to your proto message format correctly, replacing hexadecimal strings with base64 strings:

func (m *HexBytesJSONPb) Unmarshal(data []byte, v interface{}) error {
    msg, ok := v.(proto.Message)
    if !ok {
        return fmt.Errorf("expected proto.Message, got %T", v)
    }

    var jsonMap map[string]interface{}
    if err := json.Unmarshal(data, &jsonMap); err != nil {
        return err
    }

    m.overrideHexToBase64(msg.ProtoReflect(), jsonMap)

    normalizedJSON, err := json.Marshal(jsonMap)
    if err != nil {
        return err
    }

    return protojson.UnmarshalOptions{
        DiscardUnknown: true,
    }.Unmarshal(normalizedJSON, msg)
}

Step 5: Create a New Service Mux

Finally, instantiate your custom marshaler in your gRPC service:

func newCustomServMux() *runtime.ServeMux {
    return runtime.NewServeMux(runtime.WithMarshalerOption(runtime.MIMEWildcard, &HexBytesJSONPb{}))
}

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

1. Is there better documentation about custom Marshalers?

While the official documentation can be lacking, community forums and GitHub discussions often provide helpful insights and code examples.

2. Is using with custom marshaling a valid approach?

Yes, leveraging effectively differentiates which fields require special handling and maintains your API's extensibility and clarity.

Creating a custom marshaler for gRPC in Go can be initially challenging, especially when you're new to the language. However, by following these steps, you can maintain the integrity of your data format, ensuring a clear and effective communication line between services. With a combination of hexadecimal and base64 encoding, you can achieve a more readable and usable API. Keep exploring the community and resources to continue enhancing your knowledge on gRPC with Go!

items.title: Journal Entry: When Overengineering Bit Me
items.author: Rhaqim
items.content:
Overengineering is a silent productivity killer.

I recently found myself debugging an application that kept hanging. On the surface, everything looked fine: no panics, no major memory spikes just random, hard-to-reproduce blocking behaviour. It was frustrating.

Eventually, I discovered the root cause: I had hundreds of goroutines sleeping silently in the background.

  • After sending a verification email, I launched a goroutine to sleep for 10 minutes and then update the database to clear the token.
  • Each user action spun up a new goroutine.
  • I wasn’t tracking or cancelling these goroutines. No context, no lifecycle awareness.
  • As if that wasn’t bad enough, the goroutines were all hitting a relational database to update tokens that only lived for a few minutes.

At first, it felt like the “Go” thing to do. Using goroutines and sleep timers to delay token deletion. But in reality, I had:

  • Increased DB load for ephemeral data.
  • Ignored lifecycle cleanup.

Surely there had to be a better and overall cleaner way to achieve this goal, so, I stepped back and I asked myself: “Do I really need to store short-lived verification tokens in a full-blown SQL database?”. The answer was obvious. (The answer is no, by the way. In case you weren’t sure.

Here’s how I fixed the bug:

  • I moved token storage to Redis with a simple TTL.
  • Removed all background goroutines tied to token expiration.
  • Simplified the code and reduced the mental load of managing side effects.

And just like that, after rocking back and forth wide-eyed for days like a crazy person in front of my computer, the blocking issues disappeared. Redis cleaned up expired keys automatically, and my backend was leaner and more predictable.

A major takeaway from this experience is that the simplest solutions are often the most effective.

If you’re using goroutines for background jobs with sleep timers, ask yourself:

Do I need this process to live beyond the request lifecycle?, Should this be a scheduled job instead?, Can Redis or another TTL-based store simplify this logic?

It’s fine to build fast but it’s much better to build wisely. That’s how you keep your sanity intact.

items.title: 🐹 Series: Leetcoding in Go — slices.Backward()
items.author: Kyle Grah
items.content:
As Go engineers, we take pride in the language and our experience with it. Yet, many Go developers default to Python in technical interviews, often believing it's more interview-friendly.

But if you're applying to a Go-heavy team, there's no better way to win over your future coworkers than by showing you're fluent in Go — even under pressure.

There's a common perception that interviewing in Go puts you at a disadvantage compared to interviewing in Python. Python has built-ins such as reversed()—great for saving time and lines of code.

  • Tracks frequencies in a single map pass
  • Groups numbers into frequency “buckets”
  • Uses slices.Backward() to iterate from highest frequency down
  • Stops early once k results are collected 
import "slices"

func topKFrequent(nums []int, k int) []int {
    frequencies := make(map[int]int)

    for _, n := range nums {
        frequencies[n]++
    }

    buckets := make([][]int, len(nums)+1)
    for n, freq := range frequencies {
        buckets[freq] = append(buckets[freq], n)
    }

    results := make([]int, 0, k) 

// Reverse-iterate the buckets slice. Comparable to Python's reversed.
    for _, b := range slices.Backward(buckets) {
        if len(results) == k {
            return results
        }

        if len(b) <= k-len(results) {
            results = append(results, b...)
        } else {
            results = append(results, b[:k-len(results)]...)
        }
    }

    return results
}

Compared to for i := len(nums) - 1; i >= 0; i-- {}, using slices.Backward() is easier to read, more expressive of intent, and shows fluency with Go’s modern standard library.
